topic: A tenant receives an eviction notice
raw_output:
[story 1]

The crisp white paper landed on the worn kitchen table with a sound like a gunshot. Elias stared at the stark lettering, his jaw tightening until his molars ached. He picked up the notice, his knuckles white against the cheap paper, and began to trace the lines with a rigid finger. "This is absurd," he muttered, the words tasting like ash in his mouth. He pushed the paper away, the movement sharp and jerky. He paced the small living room three times, his boots scuffing the linoleum, his breath coming in short, shallow gasps. He looked out the window at the neighboring apartment, a picture of serene, undisturbed living, and a hot, prickly feeling rose up in his chest, making him clench his fists so tightly his nails dug crescent moons into his palms. He didn't bother to read the fine print; he just wanted to rip the whole thing in half.

[story 2]

When the mail slot rattled, Maya snatched the envelope with a sudden, violent movement. Her eyes scanned the official document, and a slow, dangerous flush spread across her neck. She didn't drop it; instead, she held it aloft, letting the harsh fluorescent light catch the formal typeface. "Are you serious?" she asked the empty room, her voice dangerously level, though the tremor in her hands betrayed the storm brewing inside. She dropped the paper back onto the counter, but didn't place it gently. It landed with a deliberate thud. She walked to the window, pressing her forehead against the cool glass, letting out a slow, controlled exhale that was more of a suppressed snarl. Every nerve ending felt hyper-alert, buzzing with a furious, trapped energy. This was an insult, a complete disregard for everything she had built here.

[story 3]

He read the notice twice, his gaze flicking back and forth between the dates and the demand, a muscle ticking visibly in his temple. A low growl started deep in his throat, a sound he barely managed to swallow down. He slammed his hand flat onto the wooden desk, the resulting vibration rattling a nearby stack of old magazines. "Unbelievable," he hissed, his voice tight and strained. He leaned over the desk, his shadow engulfing the document, and slowly, deliberately, he crumpled the paper into a tight, furious ball. He didn't throw it; he simply squeezed it until the fibers groaned under the pressure, his whole body vibrating with barely contained frustration. He stood up, shaking his head slowly, a deep sense of outrage settling over him like a heavy cloak.
